questions.scenario:
A peering request arrives from team Atlas:

```text
Current estate:
vpc-prod       10.0.0.0/16
vpc-analytics  10.1.0.0/16

Atlas request:
atlas-edge     10.0.128.0/17
```

What is the correct review result?

options.text: Reject it because `10.0.128.0/17` sits inside the existing `10.0.0.0/16` range
Approve it because a `/17` can never overlap a `/16`
Approve it because AWS checks only for exact CIDR equality
Reject it only if Atlas uses a public IP range

questions.explanation: A `/16` covers every address from `10.0.0.0` through `10.0.255.255`. The requested `/17` is a subset of that range, so peering would create ambiguous routing.

questions.scenario:
An on-prem VPN proposal includes this new route:

```text
onprem-containers 172.17.10.0/24
```

Your platform inventory says many hosts use Docker's default bridge network `172.17.0.0/16`. What should you flag?

options.text: The proposal overlaps the Docker bridge range and can collide with container networking on those hosts
The proposal is public internet space, so overlap checks do not apply
The proposal is safe because `/24` networks cannot overlap `/16` networks
The proposal is safe because all `172.x.x.x` addresses are outside private networking

questions.explanation: The article calls out `172.16.0.0/12` as private space and mentions Docker's default `172.17.0.0/16`. A `/24` inside that `/16` overlaps it even though the prefix lengths differ.

questions.scenario:
A production VPC request copies these CIDRs from an example document:

```text
api-vpc      192.0.2.0/24
worker-vpc   198.51.100.0/24
edge-vpc     203.0.113.0/24
```

What is the best review comment?

options.text: Reject them because these are documentation ranges and should not appear in real production configuration
Approve them because documentation ranges are reserved for private cloud VPCs
Approve only `203.0.113.0/24` because it is the largest private range
Reject them because `/24` is smaller than the AWS minimum subnet size

questions.explanation: The article lists `192.0.2.0/24`, `198.51.100.0/24`, and `203.0.113.0/24` as documentation ranges. Seeing them in production usually means someone copied examples without replacing the values.

questions.scenario:
Your current routed inventory is:

```text
prod-core       10.0.0.0/16
prod-shared     10.2.0.0/16
corp-vpn        172.16.0.0/12
```

Which proposed ranges overlap the current inventory?
questions.explanation: `10.0.64.0/18` is inside `10.0.0.0/16`, `172.20.5.0/24` is inside `172.16.0.0/12`, and `10.2.128.0/17` is inside `10.2.0.0/16`. The other proposed ranges may be private, but they do not overlap the inventory shown.

questions.scenario:
Atlas also requests these CIDRs:

```text
10.50.0.0/16
10.51.0.0/16
192.168.100.0/24
```

The current estate contains only `10.0.0.0/16`, `10.1.0.0/16`, and `10.2.0.0/16`. Why are these not the blocking ranges in this inventory?

options.text: They do not intersect the three existing routed ranges, even though they are private address space
They are public address ranges, so peering ignores them
They are safe because every `/16` is automatically non-overlapping with every other `/16`
They are safe because `192.168.0.0/16` can never be used in a cloud VPC

questions.explanation: Overlap checks compare actual address ranges. The listed Atlas ranges are private, but they do not fall inside `10.0.0.0/16`, `10.1.0.0/16`, or `10.2.0.0/16`.

questions.scenario:
A host in subnet `10.0.0.0/20` is configured as follows:

```text
address 10.0.1.50
mask    255.255.255.0
peer    10.0.2.100
```

Traffic to the peer takes a strange gateway path or fails intermittently. Which subnetting mistake best explains it?

options.text: The host mask is `/24`, so it wrongly treats `10.0.2.100` as outside the local subnet even though the real subnet is `/20`
The peer is outside `10.0.0.0/20`, so the gateway path is always correct
The mask is too large because `/24` includes every address in `10.0.0.0/16`
The problem is DNS, because IP masks do not affect routing decisions

questions.explanation: The article's wrong-mask failure mode uses this exact pattern: a host configured with a narrower mask sends traffic to the default gateway even when the destination belongs in the actual local subnet.

questions.scenario: A manager asks why peering cannot just prefer the more specific route if two VPCs both use `10.0.0.0/16`. Which explanation is most accurate for the review?

options.text: Overlapping CIDRs make ownership ambiguous for destinations inside the shared range, so the safe fix is non-overlapping allocation or renumbering
Peering works with overlaps as long as both VPCs use private RFC 1918 space
The router can always infer ownership from the VPC name in the DNS record
Overlaps matter only for IPv6, not IPv4

questions.explanation: The article states that overlapping CIDRs break peering and routing because routers cannot decide which network owns a destination address. There is no shortcut other than planning non-overlapping ranges or renumbering one side.
